stonetop.summary: In the time of the Makers, the Green Lords ruled the Great Wood and what is now Ferrier’s Fen.

text.content:
In the time of @UUID[Compendium.stonetop_pwd.stonetop-journal.JournalEntry.eoTIBDMIzJXByPCZ]{the Makers}, the Green Lords ruled @UUID[Compendium.stonetop_pwd.stonetop-journal.JournalEntry.6kt1b8ozEREDCi4k]{the Great Wood} and what is now @UUID[Compendium.stonetop_pwd.stonetop-journal.JournalEntry.UstvXnD6EA9IBBO3]{Ferrier’s Fen}. They grew things, bred and cultivated. They bent the spirits of wild to their will, and brought forth new forms of life—some of which would eventually become @UUID[Compendium.stonetop_pwd.stonetop-journal.JournalEntry.wxslMExj4dQkTPfw]{the Forest Folk}.

Yet while they mastered life itself, they feared death. They dreaded the bleak eternity waiting behind the Last Door. They bred @UUID[Compendium.stonetop_pwd.stonetop-journal.JournalEntry.Ej5WiMs30baKNXW3]{the Fae} out of spirit and flesh, and harnessed their power to craft false afterlives. And: it worked. Dozens of Green Lords had themselves mummified and entombed, dreaming forever of decadence and wonder.

Some of the Fae, however, grew to resent their servitude. They plotted in secret and rose up against their masters. They gutted tombs, put the living to the sword, and sent the survivors running.

The Green Lords, in panic and desperation, called on @UUID[Compendium.stonetop_pwd.stonetop-journal.JournalEntry.5Kym1qDHTmM8XUQ6]{the Things Below} and used their power to quash the rebellion. But this in turn drew the ire of other Makers. They worked a mighty magic that collapsed a vast swath of Green Lord territory, sinking it into a mire and killing thousands. The Fae rallied and besieged the survivors at @UUID[Compendium.stonetop_pwd.stonetop-journal.JournalEntry.76FKkdtTFxWEGIKQ]{Vor Svetelik}. Their cause lost, the Green Lords unleashed a last great wave of horror before retreating into the bowels of the earth. There they have remained, growing ever more twisted and vile, becoming @UUID[Compendium.stonetop_pwd.stonetop-journal.JournalEntry.lB57gclVP4TiOIRb]{the Fomoraij}.
Slavic-sounding: Athanasi, Bogdana, Chedomir, Dozhena, Darobor, Jigorii, Kresimira, Mstislava, Nikalor, Radomira, Tikomar, Tomila, Vecheslav, Voitsek, Yaroslana, Yevena.

• An earthquake or wildfire reveals a long-buried Green Lord ruin close to @UUID[Compendium.stonetop_pwd.stonetop-journal.JournalEntry.6yScslDfqrcCQ6CJ]{Stonetop}.
  • A hazardous growth is aggressively spreading through @UUID[Compendium.stonetop_pwd.stonetop-journal.JournalEntry.6kt1b8ozEREDCi4k]{the Great Wood}
  • An old scholar or manuscript shows up, telling of a Green Lord relic that can greatly improve crops.
  • Some fool braves a Green Lord’s tomb and returns with a valuable treasure, but they’re pursued by mummified servants and infected with a horrible rotting disease.

Pick, or roll 1d12.

RollResult
1Beasts or vermin: bred, changed, or harnessed
2Plants and growing things: grandiose and/or unusual
3Adaptation/growth/ expansion/vitality
4Chimerae: creatures with disparate parts, features of other organisms
5Tombs, mummification, constructed afterlives
6The spirits of the wild or that which they embody, bound or put to use
7@UUID[Compendium.stonetop_pwd.stonetop-journal.JournalEntry.Ej5WiMs30baKNXW3]{Fae} servants/rebellion
8Corruption by @UUID[Compendium.stonetop_pwd.stonetop-journal.JournalEntry.5Kym1qDHTmM8XUQ6]{the Things Below}, and roll again
9–12Sized for giants, and roll 1d8 again

Pick, or have someone roll the Die of Fate.

1d6 site

1-4 Lingering signs of their presence and influence (at right) 5-6 A ruin

Lingering signs Pick, or have someone roll the Die of Fate.

1d6 sign

RollResult
1An entity, imprisoned in a strange tree, rune-carved stone, or natural feature
2Strange plants
3A section of the Broken Roads, unearthed or still barely visible
4A spirit of the wild, bound to some useful purpose
5Useful or valuable flora growing in abundance
6A waystone

An entity, imprisoned For example:

  • A tree with a metallic sheen, its wood infused with iron. Its trunk spirals around itself, like a spool of yarn, a rebellious @UUID[Compendium.stonetop_pwd.stonetop-journal.JournalEntry.Ej5WiMs30baKNXW3]{Fae} trapped within.
  • A man-sized mass of blood-sucking insects—an entity of pain and disease—imprisoned in amber.
  • A mighty old elder tree, its bark like bone, oozing black sap from the deathless horror it holds bound in its roots.
  • A great hunk of stone, reminiscent of a ravenous, howling beast, wrapped in petrified vines and roots and mostly buried/submerged.
  • A Fae locked in the form of a tree, a snarl of defiance barely visible.

Strange plants For example:

  • A grove of trees, planted in strangely regular patterns (neat rows, spirals, etc.)
  • Trees/vines, their trunks or stalks woven around each other to form walls, curtains, ceilings, etc.
  • Shrubs/briars in crude topiary shapes with no obvious tending
  • Grand sculptures of living wood that memorialize long-dead Green Lords
  • Moss, lichen, mold, or fungus that glows, or hums, or gives off heat, etc.
  • Plants made partially of impossible material (flowers/leaves of glass, bark infused with metal, a vine with thorns of bone, a petrified tree that blooms with normal flowers, etc.)
  • An enormous and ancient tree, festooned with gylglyd vines that protect some Green Lord artifact.

A spirit of the wild, bound For example:

  • A spirit of winter/hibernation bound to a grotto by vines growing in runic patterns. Other runes fill those who approach with dread. An ancient being lies in torpor within.
  • A clearing or meadow lush with an edible grain, tended by a fertility spirit bound to a menhir standing in the field’s center.
  • An orchard of fruit trees, guarded by a birdwaig and her glasbren daughters, a long family of spirits and trees bound by an ancient oath to the Green Lords.
  • A ring of five ancient willow trees, each with a rune-carved hollow, surrounding a stretch of mire. A spirit of reclamation and decay is bound within. Anything that enters the mire will soon be sucked into the muck, where it will decompose and dissolve at an alarming rate.
  • A huge stone altar on a treeless hilltop, exposed to the sun. A spirit of growth and vitality is bound within, fed by blood and bestowing gifts of strength.
  • An obelisk of milky white crystal, pushing out of the loamy soil, shot through with tendrils of vine that form looping runes of binding. The spirit bound within can pull forth maladies and afflictions from those who touch it, but the spirit is willful and the process is excruciating.

Ruins Pick or roll for theme, structure, purpose, architectural elements, and condition. Place it in an appropriate terrain (see page 202 for @UUID[Compendium.stonetop_pwd.stonetop-journal.JournalEntry.6kt1b8ozEREDCi4k]{the Great Wood} or page 118 for @UUID[Compendium.stonetop_pwd.stonetop-journal.JournalEntry.UstvXnD6EA9IBBO3]{Ferrier’s Fen}). Populate it with lingering signs, artifacts and/or dangers of the Green Lords or the appropriate region.

1d6 structure

RollResult
1Sprawling compound of “smaller” buildings
2Single, smaller building
3Earth-covered mound
4Ancient trees, rooms and structures built into hollows and on crooks
5Underground vault(s)
6Ziggurat/pyramid/dome

1d6 purpose

RollResult
1Dwelling (home, barracks, dormitory, etc.)
2Containment (fortress, prison, kennel, menagerie, etc.)
3Storage (cellar, vault, warehouse, library, collection, etc.)
4Production (workshop, nursery, factory, kitchen, abattoir, etc.)
5Esoteric (laboratory, shrine, surgery, etc.)
6Tomb (of a Green Lord, their ancestors, their servants, their creations, etc.)

1d6 architectural elements

RollResult
1Huge slabs of smooth stone (white marble, granite, serpentine) carved with scrollwork and bas reliefs
2Moss and lichen, of striking colors, growing in decorative patterns
3Amber, agate, geodes, even unworked boulders of interesting shape
4Ivory, bone, horn, antler, fur, silk— sometimes fused with or growing from wood or stone
5Pillars, arches, or even walls of petrified wood, seemingly grown into shape
6Vines and tendrils (often petrified or metal-infused) clinging to surfaces in branching whorls, sigils, and runes

Most Maker-ruins are sized for giants— people two or even three times as tall as humans—but often with smaller-scale rooms, passages, and so forth for their servants.

1d6 condition

RollResult
1–2Shattered and laid waste by time, war, or vandalism; mostly buried, submerged, and/or overgrown; its purpose unrecognizable
3–4Crumbling, largely buried, submerged, and/or overgrown
5Buried, submerged, and/or overgrown, but mostly intact
6Fully visible and strangely well-preserved

See @UUID[Compendium.stonetop_pwd.stonetop-journal.JournalEntry.76FKkdtTFxWEGIKQ]{Vor Svetelik} (the ruined city of the Green Lords, page 468) for additional examples.

Many Green Lord ruins feature (or are preserved by) Fae domains.

A crumbling ziggurat, half-collapsed and mostly covered with dirt, trees, and webs. Inside is a smaller ziggurat that bears a silk tapestry of the Mother-of-Spiders, a powerful elemental spirit. In days of yore, this place was filled with spiders of all sizes, spinning silk and weaving impossible fabrics for the Green Lords. Now, the spiders merely grow large, and hungry, and strangely intelligent.

The PCs are tracking nailadd (giant jumping spiders) through the Great Wood, and I think that they’re heading for a Green Lord ruin. I roll a 6 for theme (the spirits of the wild, bound or put to use), a 6 for structure (ziggurat/pyramid/dome), a 4 for purpose (production), a 4 for architectural elements (ivory, bone, horn, antler, fur, silk), and 3 for condition (crumbling, largely buried and overgrown). I ponder for a little, then come up with this:

Various treasures

  • A mummified bird in a clay pot (fragile, Value 0)
  • A number of corroded bronze hatchets of ancient make, with beautifully preserved wooden grips (crude and Value 0 as-is; hand and Value 1 if restored)
  • A beautiful silk shirt, embroidered with vine-like patterns (Value 1)
  • Stacks of raw, unworked timber with a metallic sheen to the grain; incredibly strong but incredibly difficult to work (immobile, Value 2)
  • A coat of scale armor for someone 20 feet tall. The scales are strange, organic, semi-translucent and iridescent around the edges. Thick, twisting vines grow from the armor where a body should be; nothing else grows nearby. The armor is impractical and creepy, but if extracted intact, it could fetch a decent price (immobile, Value 2) to a collector.
  • An oversized drinking horn, gold and amber fused into the horn in impossible ways (Value 3)
  • A tall, thick, gnarled tree bearing large, delicious fruits in early autumn. Provides as many provisions (4 uses) as the PCs care to take, and could be cultivated into a rhoillyg orchard.
  • A large urn immobile, magical When the urn is unoccupied and a @UUID[Compendium.stonetop_pwd.stonetop-journal.JournalEntry.Ej5WiMs30baKNXW3]{Fae} looks inside, it is compelled to crawl in (it can always fit). If you then close the lid, the Fae is trapped and the urn slowly and painfully peels away its knowledge, essence, and sense of self. In time, nothing remains but a pile of pearls (Value 2).

. steading improvement .

RHOILLYG ORCHARD Requires both:

  • A sack full of rhoillyg seeds
  • An herbalist of considerable skill and patience

And either of these, to germinate the seeds:

  • A year or so of experimentation
  • Advice from a knowledgeable source

And then each of these:

  • A full spring, to sprout and cultivate saplings
  • Pulling Together once, over the course of a summer, to clear and plant the orchard
  • Protecting the orchard through two more summers

When you mark all the requirements, increase Fortunes by 1 and add “Rhoillyg Orchard” to both the Resources list and the steading map. Henceforth, the steading generates +1 Surplus in summer and another +1 Surplus when the autumn harvest is complete.

  • A vine in a rune-carved pot
  • , magical It's about as thick as your finger and maybe 30 feet long stretched out, with skinny little tendrils. It looks rather dried out, but moves slightly if you touch it or its pot. If you water it, it twitches excitedly.
  • A fang-dagger hand, magical, +1 damage An 8-inch fang, bound to a wooden hilt by fiber that never rots. A cruel-looking thing, with strange carvings along the blade.
  • Something interesting: The fang looks like it came from a cynddaraig, and the markings are definitely some sort of spell related to blood and soil.
  • Something useful: When you bury the fang-dagger and water the soil with blood, the ground buckles and bulges. A minute later, a full-grown cynddaraig bursts from the soil—angry, confused, and hungry. The spell grants no control over the beast and provides no way to dismiss it. The dagger is gone, now a tooth in the rage-drake’s maw.
  • A big stone coffer immobile, magical
  • Carved from serpentine, 4 feet by 2 feet by 2, with a heavy stone lid. When found, it’s likely half-full with soil. To those sensitive to such things, it thrums with the power of fertility and growth.

When you add fresh soil to the coffer, plant seeds in it, and close the lid, the seeds will germinate and sprout overnight. Each seed will sprout only once, and the sprouts henceforth grow at a regular rate, regardless of whether they stay in the coffer or are planted elsewhere.

With coordination, this could lead to a boom in @UUID[Compendium.stonetop_pwd.stonetop-journal.JournalEntry.6yScslDfqrcCQ6CJ]{Stonetop}’s gardens and +1 Surplus each summer. In the hands of a skilled herbalist, this can greatly improve an herb garden and allow the cultivation of rare or difficult plants. The herbalists of @UUID[Compendium.stonetop_pwd.stonetop-journal.JournalEntry.uXlyry9CpXUz4ooR]{Marshedge} would kill over this—or even start a war.

  • Small clay jars fragile, magical A number of earthenware jars, about the size of a pinecone, sealed with wax and seemingly empty. When opened (or broken) a fine cloud of dust puffs out. The dust is, in fact, chogor spores.

When someone is exposed to chogor spores, the spores quickly start to grow on them in masses of vine-like ferns. They will—in a matter of seconds—ensnare and restrain their victim. After about an hour, the ferns die and rapidly dry out, but until then, it’s like being bound up in twine.

  • Minor arcana
  • Pick 1 or have someone roll.

1d6 minor arcanum

RollResult
1A strange skull and antlers
2A giant oak leaf
3A huge wooden sphere
4A stretched vellum
5A patch of rainbow moss
6A crumbling arch
  • Major arcana
  • These might very well be associated with the Green Lords.
  • @UUID[Compendium.stonetop_pwd.stonetop-items.Item.0n4lgBdJ0abmmQfl]{The Twisted Spear}
  • @UUID[Compendium.stonetop_pwd.stonetop-items.Item.PVOSwDruAGSeUI3V]{The Shield of the Wisent Witch}

Bound Entities

  • Spirits of the wild, forced or enticed to perform a particular task or to protect a particular place
  • @UUID[Compendium.stonetop_pwd.stonetop-journal.JournalEntry.Ej5WiMs30baKNXW3]{Fae}, loyal and still serving their embalmed masters (possibly bitter and questioning their fate) or rebellious and imprisoned for their crimes
  • Spirits and beasts corrupted by @UUID[Compendium.stonetop_pwd.stonetop-journal.JournalEntry.5Kym1qDHTmM8XUQ6]{the Things Below}, either locked away by the Green Lords, or weaponized against the Fae
  • Plasmic horrors, which once served as blank canvases for fleshweaving
  • Chimerae
  • The Green Lords meddled wantonly with life, mixing creatures in impossible ways to suit need or whim. Some such creatures linger, breeding true or having survived for hundreds of years.

Pick or roll for purpose, ability, size, and composition. Allow purpose, ability, and size to inform the elements of its composition, and what traits it has from each element.

RollResult
1–2War/destruction/murder/hunting
3–4Defense/protection/companionship
5–6Experimentation/insight/curiosity
7–8Labor/industry/mundane tasks/ convenience
9–10Something obscure, or very specific
11–12Roll 1d10 twice and combine

1d12 ability

RollResult
1Burn/dissolve/destroy/drain
2Transform/mutate/change
3Control/influence minds or emotions
4Wield/produce/ignore elements or energy
5Poison/disease/infect
6Hide/mimic/disguise
7Entangle/trap/engulf/subdue
8Attack at range/over an area
9Prowess/mobility/speed
10Strength/toughness/durability
11–12Roll 1d10 twice

1d12 size

  • 1-2 Cat-sized or smaller (tiny) 3-4 Like a human child (small) 5-8 Human-sized 9-11 Like a horse, a cart, etc. (large) 12 Like an elephant, or bigger (huge)
  • 1d12 composition
RollResult
1–4Beast + beast
5–6Beast + plant
7–8Beast + mineral
9–11Beast + roll again with 1d8
12Person + roll again with 1d8
  • Hazardous growth
  • Pick or roll for form and peril, possibly rolling the Die of Fate for size/scope/ scale. Maybe consider how it’s useful/ valuable as well.

1d12 form

RollResult
1–2Invisible to the naked eye/ spores/dust
3–4Mold/moss/lichen/slime
5–6Mushrooms/puffballs/stinkhorns/ fungus
7–8Vines/briars/brambles/shoots
9–10Flowers/plants/ferns/grasses
11–12Tree(s)/shrubs/bushes/hedges

1d12 peril

RollResult
1Crushes, strangles, cuts, stabs (possibly animate)
2Eats away, dissolves, absorbs (tissue or specific material)
3Grows incredibly quickly, overwhelms, ensnares
4Infects, colonizes, sickens, changes
5Poisons, weakens, subdues
6Causes dizziness, hallucinations, delusions
7Releases spores/pollen/etc. that irritate, obscure, agitate
8Draws attention (via noise, phosphorescence, scent, etc.)
9–10Roll 1d10 twice and combine
11–12Roll again with 1d10 but make it weird—elemental, psychic, spiritual, extremely specific, etc.

See @UUID[Compendium.stonetop_pwd.stonetop-journal.JournalEntry.76FKkdtTFxWEGIKQ]{Vor Svetelik} (ruined city of the Green Lords, page 468) for specific examples.

The Unquiet Dead

  • Ghosts of the Green Lords and their many servants, who died in battle or sudden cataclysm.
  • Specters of Green Lords who were murdered by trusted servants.
  • Wraiths of Green Lords who were mummified and entombed, only to have their false afterlives ruined by treachery and war.
  • Nemurvojak, the corpses of mortal servants, reanimated by demon-seed
  • until sunset
  • Leap out of the water with shocking speed
  • Follow prey from the sky, or hanging from trees
  • Strafe its prey and deliver a painful, debilitating sting
  • Chomp down, and then swallow whole

Everyone knows to shun deep, still water, and the llamudwr (or “water leapers”) are surely one reason why. Take the body of a frog, 6 or 7 feet long. Add another 8 or 9 feet of whipping tail, tipped with a venomous barb. Get rid of its legs, not even stumps, but give it bat wings, a span of nearly 20 feet unfurled. Hide it in a lake or fen, away from the sun’s cruel light. And finally, make it hungry. Very, very hungry.

  • Mummified Green Lord
  • Solitary, large, spirit, undead, magical,

They lie entombed, their corpses embalmed, their souls bound to the heavily-warded canopic jars that store their most-vital organs. Yet those souls linger forever in Fae-woven realms of glamour and delight. In these realms, the Green Lords are as gods. They are loathe to leave them, and rarely need to. But should their existence be truly threatened, they will return to this world of base flesh, inhabit their long-dead bodies, and punish any intruders with the savagery of the cruelest beasts.

Some of the Green Lords were mummified and interred with their most faithful mortal servants. These servants get to enjoy their master’s false afterlife, but when threats to the tomb arise, they are sent back to their corpses, to rise and crush any intruders they find.

Hunched humanoid forms carved of wood, with great wings, taloned feet, and heads crowned with branch-like antlers. They were made by the Green Lords to be sentinels and guardians, to watch over their borders and their most important sites. Now, ages and generations later, they still watch, and wait, and protect their nests.

Something interesting: The tales say that the song of a redbreasted bluebird will keep the rhagedn still, letting folk pass unmolested.

Something useful: It just so happens that you know how to whistle the tune (whether or not you can whistle it well enough is questionable).

Swyn are great serpents with simian heads and hypnotic eyes. The Green Lords bred them out of desperation, borrowing secrets from the Things Below. They wanted spymasters and saboteurs. What they got was a race of petty, craven narcissists.

Each swyn fancies itself the greatest, the most beautiful, the most magnificent of beings. They have exacting tastes, far more subtle and refined than any of their peers. But should you embarrass, defy, or simply bore one, expect to be brutally devoured. Few of these creatures survive, thankfully. The Fae hunt them and they shun others of their kind, jealous of territory and wealth. But every dozen years or so, some urge drives them all to the ruins of Vor Svetelik, entourage in tow, where they engage in ostentatious displays and mating rituals. There, amidst the sick white trees, the next brood of swyn are seeded. There, perhaps, these foul things might be wiped out once and for all.

When the swyn compels you to act against your will, mark XP if you do what it wants. If you resist, you are Defying Danger.

When the Fae rebelled, the Green Lords made living weapons to use against them. The zrajedak—or “treachery-eaters”—were one such weapon. They merged the body of a lion, the tail of a spiney drake, and the face of their mortal servants, and then infused it all with iron, and malice, and the cruel power of the Things Below.

Some of these horrors are trapped by Fae magic. Others remain in stasis, yet to be unleashed on the world. But surely some few stalk the world still, for @UUID[Compendium.stonetop_pwd.stonetop-journal.JournalEntry.wxslMExj4dQkTPfw]{the Forest Folk} would tell hushed tales of them. “Never trust someone who will not show you their teeth,” they’d say, “for Zrajedak smiles only before he bites.”

Creatures the bestiary catalogues for Green Lords. Click through for full stat blocks and lore.

  • @UUID[Compendium.stonetop_pwd.stonetop-journal.JournalEntry.7qfPIuaRwuUPi4nV]{Llamudwr}

  • @UUID[Compendium.stonetop_pwd.stonetop-journal.JournalEntry.bO6QXFMmtAmKL3zO]{Mummified Servant}
  • @UUID[Compendium.stonetop_pwd.stonetop-journal.JournalEntry.h9ZdkjGYXgOAOhx8]{Rhagedn}
  • @UUID[Compendium.stonetop_pwd.stonetop-journal.JournalEntry.k9Buk0QDWg2PIIqj]{Swyn}
  • @UUID[Compendium.stonetop_pwd.stonetop-journal.JournalEntry.CGPfnYi6Y86dEdE0]{Zrajedak}
